narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because a model flagged a 49.8% chance of not passing the bar exam feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why it landed there, like sharing the story behind a tough coach's decision. Picture your profile coming together in the model's eyes: your Year 3 decile of 1 stood out as the biggest red flag, signaling that even among your peers, your performance dipped to the very bottom, pulling hardest toward this failure prediction. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 27 whispered doubts about your raw analytical edge compared to others who ace those tests. Your Year 1 decile of 2 wasn't much brighter, adding to the sense of an early struggle that didn't fully turn around. Coming from a middle-income family, being Asian, and identifying as male each nudged the picture a bit grimmer, as the model saw patterns where folks like you faced steeper odds in this arena. Your undergrad GPA of 3.0 and full-time student status rounded out a profile that, to the model, looked like someone grinding against the current more than riding the wave. It's not that any one thing doomed you—your undergraduate record showed solid effort—but this particular mix painted you as higher risk for the bar's brutal pass rate. The good news? This is just one snapshot; with targeted prep on those late-year grades or LSAT-level reasoning, you could rewrite your story for the next opportunity. You've got the drive—full-time commitment says that much—and that's what truly turns predictions around. Hang in there; law school's gate isn't sealed forever.
